data.title: Kinder Morgan: I've Had Positive Realizations, But Valuation And Technical Caution Remain
data.description:
The oil and gas market has been volatile throughout the year, driven by their extreme price swings, geopolitical tensions in Ukraine-Russia and the Middle East, and even stubborn inflation. Yet, they still show a lot of growth catalysts today. That is why I somehow understand why Kinder Morgan, Inc. (KMI) still increased and delivered 22% returns despite my cautious rating in my initial coverage. Even so, I am still firm with hold rating as valuation becomes more elevated. Technicals now appear more synchronized and adherent because the stock has already reached the overselling threshold.

Kinder Morgan: How It Has Been Lately

Natural gas and oil prices have fluctuated wildly in the past year due to various interlocking supply-demand dynamics. Despite these swings, Kinder Morgan, Inc. was able to benefit from the recent situations. Although its overall operations are not directly tied to energy commodity prices, the positive spillovers are poured into it. This was shown in its most recent performance.

In Q4 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $4.5B, up by 13.0% YoY from $4.0B. This YoY revenue growth was higher than in my previous coverage at 11.2%, which shows its sustained growth. One factor to consider is strong demand, as KMI continued to receive contracts, projects, or agreements. These are typically fee-based in fixed, so short-term swings do not immediately disrupt their metrics like volume and pricing. Note that it had $10B in project backlog after adding $912M of projects while placing about $265M in service. I think this should not be surprising since the natural gas rebounded and exceeded the $5 per MMBtu mark before the quarter ended. Higher prices mean higher profits for natural gas producers. In turn, production rose, which increased the demand for midstream services, such as transportation, gathering, and distribution. You can see the increased volume in its natural gas pipelines. The same applies to its oil segment. Because oil prices were lower, its oil midstream volume decreased as well. But because its exposure is mainly natural gas, its revenues remained solid.

Likewise, the operating costs and expenses increased in line with its increased capacity. But they remained manageable at 9.0%, showing its improved efficiency. In the end, its operating margin increased to 30.2% versus 27.7% YoY.

Middle East Tension: A Bear and Bull Case

If you had asked me about the civil unrest in Iran two months ago, I would have said that it would not escalate and disrupt the global market. But things totally changed when the US and Israel intervened, which led to Iran's retaliation and attack on neighboring nations. The blockade on the Strait of Hormuz has already sent oil prices to their highs. And I think this will be a double-edged sword for the oil and gas market, including KMI.

On the bear side, if the tension escalates and affects multiple sectors, a recession may take place and may lower energy demand. Production volumes may decrease, leading to lower midstream volumes. LNG project delays are also possible since the tension can slow US LNG exports, which may lower its cash flow.

On the other hand, KMI is based in the US, so its pipelines won't be automatically hurt. As the global supply tightens, many consumers may shift their natural gas and oil demand to the US. This may lead to more production volumes and LNG exports. So, there may also be more volumes through its pipelines. Another is that the US is a major energy exporter, so it is not dependent on the Middle East. KMI's physical assets, like pipelines, are not affected by the conflict. Instead, it may benefit from it since higher oil prices may motivate oil producers and increase midstream volumes.

Why KMI is Well-Positioned

Amid the mixed impact of the recent market trends, I believe that KMI is still strategically positioned.

One aspect to consider is its business model. Its fee-based contract business model allows it to secure stable revenue streams regardless of market fluctuations. It will always receive fixed fees to move gas or liquids. With that, customers will still pay even if they don't use full capacity. This lets KMI maintain predictable cash flow and lower its sensitivity to oil and gas price swings. More importantly, these are typically contracts for a specified period, which mostly last for several years. With that, KMI has visibility on future income and cash flow. Of course, this still has downside risks because it is capital-intensive, with growth tied deeply to new projects. No project means no revenue. But with its established market presence, I believe that it already has a strong network.

Natural gas may be its main driver due to its growth potential in the succeeding years. This may be driven by the global data center expansion. Maybe the expansion may slow down if the tension escalates and leads to a recession. But in the long run, I believe that its expansion is sure. Currently, the power demand of data centers in the US is about 50 GW. But by 2030, it is anticipated to more than double from current levels. I believe that KMI is ready for it. In fact, it has already begun its construction of its Trident Intrastate Pipeline to provide natural gas transportation services from Katy, Texas, to Port Arthur, Texas.

Liquidity: The Good and the Bad

Having robust liquidity is important for KMX since it can tell us how well it manages its resources and how it can sustain its expansion and dividends. Right now, I am still a bit concerned about its debt of $32B, although this is not surprising for a capital-intensive business like KMI. This is too high versus its cash and cash equivalents of $63M. That's less than one percent of its borrowings. This may be risky if we relate it to the current macroeconomic environment. The Fed may halt the policy easing cycle or even retract it by raising the interest rate again if inflation intensifies in line with the skyrocketing oil prices. This can lead to a higher borrowing cost.

On a lighter note, most of the amount will not mature within a year. To be more specific, 83% of the total borrowings has a maturity of five years, which you can see in its maturity table on pages 91-92 in its 10-K report. This is still in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of 4.4x, which means that it only needs 4.4 years theoretically to repay the whole amount even if it doesn't have to. This proves that it still has enough capital buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

Valuation: I'm Not Buying Yet

If you look at its price ratios, KMI appears to be trading at an elevated level relative to previous years. In P/E, the stock trades at 24.84x its EPS, which can be considered moderately expensive by default. It trades a bit lower than the current S&P 500's earnings multiple of 25.59x, but their narrow gap shows that KMI may be fully priced today. Also, its earnings growth in the past five years has been quite low. This gives a PEG of 2.5x (24.84/9.80), which is still quite high. Suppose its earnings growth reaches 25% this year. The PEG will decrease substantially to 0.99x (24.84/25.00). However, KMI is still fully priced at this level, which makes it hard or risky for investors to enter at this price. If the fair value is a PEG of 1.00x, then it will be $34.37, which I derived by dividing the current price by the PEG. ($34.03/0.99). Moreover, it trades above the average of 18.33x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP, or target price, will be $25.11.

I also made updates on its Dividend Discount Model. The annualized dividend is $1.17, so its dividend growth rate is 2.41% based on its five-year CAGR. Meanwhile, I set its risk premium at 5.0%, in line with the market standard. Lastly, I estimated its Beta at 0.66, which means that KMI is still less volatile than the S&P 500. With that, my TP is $22.51.

Technicals

KMI's technicals are still bullish as the price uptrend holds. It also trends above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, showing that the current momentum is still stronger than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. The 50-day SMA is also still trending upwards, which means that recent market sentiments have stayed optimistic. However, we have seen some selling pressures in the past four weeks, which suggests some caution among investors. It easily rebounded, but yesterday, the candlestick showed indecision. The long wick above the body of the candlestick showed that bulls tried to push the price up to $35 but were unsuccessful. Coincidentally, this was almost exactly the same as the market indecision last March 3 or 4 before the uptrend weakened.

Its MACD and RSI also show the same trend, although buying opportunities are still present. The MACD line is increasing again after its sharp downtrend, which shows returning buying volume. But it's still below the signal line, while the histogram remains within positive territory, which implies that selling volume still outpaces buying volume. Meanwhile, RSI shows that the downtrend was due to the recent overbuying that prompted profit-taking. It decreased to give buying space again. But at this level, buying space is still limited and may reach the overbought region again soon. So, I advise investors to stay on the sidelines.

Conclusion

Kinder Morgan, Inc. is a robust business with well-balanced growth and profitability, supported by its strategic business model and robust liquidity. It also has drivers to support its expansion in the long run. However, valuation stays elevated and suggests some caution, which makes it quite a bit challenging for entry at this level. Technicals adhere to it because the stock has reached the overbought threshold recently and may prompt profit-taking, so one may have to wait on the sidelines. I'm reiterating my hold rating on this stock.

data.title: Zoom: Undervalued And Underestimated - Even Without The Anthropic Stake
data.description:
Strong Foundation

Zoom Communications (ZM) is rated a Buy, with the company boasting an exceptional financial position that can offer both defensiveness against the current macro uncertainty that can push treasury yields higher, as well as significant potential to expand, with the Anthropic stake offering a bonus in case the AI boom continues.

Zoom's Q4 was relatively weak compared to the market's expectations, missing on the non-GAAP EPS estimates despite a slight beat on revenue, with 6% revenue growth in the Americas, 5% in EMEA, and 3% in APAC during Q4, and FY26 total revenue growing 4.4% (6.5% for enterprise revenue), delivering margin improvements and a 6.4% increase in free cash flow throughout the year to $1.9 billion (38.8% margin), while also reducing stock-based compensation expenses by 18%.

For FY27, Zoom expects to surpass $5 billion in revenue, estimating between $5.065 billion to $5.075 billion (an incredibly tight range, I might add), representing a ~4.15% increase at midpoint compared to last year, with an EPS of $5.77 to $5.81, for a free cash flow between $1.7 billion to $1.74 billion, a drop that's mostly driven by macroeconomics (interest rates falling - although the Iran conflict changed that), a one-time collections tailwinds last year and a large competitor white-labeling that churned at the end of FY26, with the management highlighting:

For FY '27, we expect free cash flow to be in the range of $1.7 billion to $1.74 billion. which includes approximately $75 million of incremental CapEx related to the post-pandemic refreshment cycle of assets across our U.S. data centers as well as similar interest income headwinds previously mentioned. As we end FY '26 and we move into FY '27, we're thrilled with our progress, and we're excited about our differentiated vision as an AI-first system of action. This success gives us confidence in our ability to grow durably beyond $5 billion in revenue across progress in meetings, continued growth in Phone, scaling our AI-first customer experience and in introducing new AI revenue streams.

Based on Zoom's latest report, we see a major advantage that I'll mention in more detail below, with an exceptional financial position, boasting zero debt and a whopping ~$7.82 billion in cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities, representing roughly 34% of the current ~$22.9 billion market cap.

Alongside the Anthropic stake that I'll mention below, Zoom's massive cash position can serve as another future catalyst, as it can allow them to make very significant investments into higher-potential areas, allowing them to deliver better than the current ~$317.25 million interest they receive.

In fact, after the failed Five9 (FIVN) all-stock $14.7 billion deal back in 2021, Zoom still acquired several smaller businesses that helped them develop their own ecosystem, including three of them (BrightHire, Bonsai, and Instamic) back in 2025.

On top of that, the company is also buying back stock of themselves, spending a massive $2.7 billion in buybacks in 2025 and 2024 ($1.62 billion and $1.09 billion, respectively), recently extending it by $1 billion, planning to at least offset the dilution for stock-based compensation in FY27, which fell to $760.78 million in FY26 compared to $931.31 million in FY25 and $1.06 billion in FY24.

Optionality From the Anthropic Stake

In what might currently be the most "under-the-radar" venture capital plays in recent tech history, given how low the current valuation is, Zoom quietly acquired an estimated $51 million stake in Anthropic after announcing a partnership to integrate their services with their Claude AI, with analysts estimating that ZM owns <1% equity in the company today.

Recent theoretical valuations such as the one from Baird sees this being worth between $2 billion to $4 billion today, with their current valuation being estimated at ~$350 billion now compared to ~$4.5 billion back when Zoom invested in them, getting in relatively before the AI valuations boom, although we are still in the industry's early stages of growth, which makes the long-term value of this stake an uncertainty - although I believe the market is still mispricing the business as a whole.

Even though the underlying business is able to make a tremendous amount of money now and should benefit from long-term digitalization trends despite lacking a major catalyst or competitive advantage against Google (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT), and others, AI integration or their AI Companion can certainly help attract or at least retain users, especially since they're using one of—if not THE—highest-quality AI models we have right now.

Major Cash Position - A "Hidden" Catalyst?

Another aspect to consider is the potential defensiveness of this stock, as it barely moved in March despite the ongoing Iran conflict and major jumps in commodity prices, although I will mention that even this can be hurt during a recession, as the market's panic can be significant.

If anything, even if we assume that the underlying business is slowly dying, there's still the potential boost thanks to their stake, while the company has virtually no debt to worry about and should even be able to benefit from a high-rate environment given the massive amount of cash on the balance sheet that's generating ~$317.25 million worth of interest per year as of 2025.

However, if this pushes the Fed into further rate cut delays or even considering rate hikes - see how Europe's borrowing costs skyrocketed to a 15-year high recently when the ECB's chief said that they're ready to hike "as early as next month" and warning about the underestimated effects on inflation that this conflict may have - that wouldn't be so terrible for Zoom, as they can capture the upside through better yields on their cash or potentially take advantage of this massive cash position to buy others at better prices and expand aggressively.

Sure, weak economic activity can be a risk to keep in mind, as that can also affect the need for Zoom's services, but the opposite can also be true, with a potential recovery in this already weak consumer environment and falling borrowing costs if the Iran situation improves, potentially leading to higher demand. Meanwhile, if this results in a resumption of the fall in treasury yields, this could also push Zoom into larger acquisitions, since holding cash would no longer be as attractive.

Meanwhile, this excellent financial position and the Anthropic stake can even make them a potential acquisition target, as we can at worst see Zoom itself as a cigar butt kind of investment with a ton of cash and the massive potential of the stake as a bonus (or even the primary reason to buy them and the business itself offering a strong cash stream even if it fades).

Risks Reiterated

Despite their fundamentals and the stake, I wouldn't ignore the potential of seeing the market treat them similarly to the broader market and potentially hurt their valuation in case of weakening market conditions.

Meanwhile, the fundamental business remains in a tough position due to the competition, as even though they're trying to offer advantages, it's still hard for them to compete with something like Google or Microsoft, who can just copy whatever works in terms of AI + Zoom or platform integration for their own services and with their own models (or OpenAI's in Microsoft's case), but also have significantly more cash to back them up and potentially outmatch Zoom - which is why a pivot may not be a terrible idea, although that also comes with risks.

Valuation

For Zoom's DCF to Enterprise Value valuation, starting from the $1.72 billion FCF guidance in 2026 and taking out roughly $320 million worth of interest income, we start with an adjusted FCF of $1.4 billion, highlighting the fundamental potential of the business itself.

I'll assume a moderate 4% CAGR over the first 5 years given their potential to expand thanks to longer-term fundamental trends and investments (nothing too big), which is not far from the market's revenue estimates, followed by a 3% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, with a high 10% WACC to take into account the competitive pressure and demand a solid margin of safety (since there's basically no debt, the cost of equity represents this percentage), with $7.82 billion in net cash and 307.33 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an enterprise value of $19.90 billion and an equity value of $27.72 billion, for a stock price of about $90.21, which is still above the current $77.71, despite the potential to see the net buybacks and higher-growth investments deliver better results.

Not only that, but you may have noticed that something big is missing: the Anthropic stake. Even at $2 billion, this could still push Zoom's valuation to roughly $96.70, although they could potentially get much more out of it.

Conclusion

I rate ZM a Buy, with an excellent financial position that offers defensiveness in case of a weak environment alongside significant potential to expand, with the Anthropic stake offering a bonus.

There is risk, of course, and this is not the Cathie Wood stock that she once predicted could hit $1,500 by 2026 in a base case, but that doesn't mean there isn't value in Zoom.

data.title: S&P 500 Earnings Update: Earnings Yield Jumps Back Over 5%
data.description:
The S&P 500 earnings yield (SP EY) jumped back over 5% on Friday, March 27, 2026 to end the week at 5.06%, the first time - per this blog’s internal spreadsheet - since early May ’25 that the S&P 500 earnings yield has been over 5%.

On 5/5/25, S&P 500 earnings yield ended that week at 5.5%, the highest S&P 500 earnings yield this blog has noted since late January ’24.

As a market-timing measure, the SP EY is an imperfect or inexact timing measure, but it should start getting readers' attention along with investor sentiment.

For historical purposes, the SP EY did trade north of 6% for most of 2023, after the FOMC started raising rates off 0% in 2022, so the SP EY can remain elevated for long periods of time.

Energy sector estimates for 2026 start to rise

Estimate growth rate source: LSEG.com

Note the jump in the expected energy sector growth for ’26 per the LSEG data as of Friday, March 27th, 2026.

Energy has largely been a dead sector since 2014, and the fact is without the Iran strikes, the world was convinced that the supply and demand for crude oil was largely in balance.

The Big Problem with Technology Valuation

Looking at the spreadsheet above, there is no problem with tech sector earnings; note the growth in “expected” ’26 tech sector earnings for full-year 2026.

Remember though, free cash flow is not earnings (EPS) and doesn’t run through the income statement. This blog post from July ’24 tried to talk to readers about the difference between EPS and free cash flow, which is one of the fundamental underpinnings of a stock’s valuation. EPS is an accounting function, and frequently contains many non-cash expenses: the basic valuation formula is, without getting too deep into the weeds (extrapolating FCF forward, discounting it back, etc.):

  • cash flow from operations
  • less capex
  • equals free cash flow

The growth in capex for the hyperscalers is so large today, it’s historically unprecedented. But that capex doesn’t show up anywhere in the income statement, but is rather capitalized and run through the cash flow statement.

Thus, while EPS growth looks tremendous for investors, the problem is tech valuations could shrink as free cash flow gets continually pushed lower as capex grows.

The goal today is not get too wonky with the Saturday morning blog.

The cold hard truth about managing money is you have to worry about everything. Will this wild capex growth eventually cause major revisions lower in tech sector valuations?

Summary / conclusion

The goal of today’s blog post was to show that the S&P 500 earnings yield has moved above the critical 5% yield level, therefore finally, the S&P 500 could be said to be getting back to a more reasonable valuation. A 5% EY could imply for some a 20x P/E for the S&P 500 benchmark, which again is more reasonable than January’s and February’s P/E valuation levels.

None of this is a recommendation or advice, but only an opinion. We are starting to get a whiff of fear in the stock and bond markets - not as bad as the 60x VIX in early April ’25 around Liberation Day - but the VIX finished the week of 3/27/26 over 30. Readers and clients should gauge their own comfort with portfolio volatility and adjust accordingly. LSEG is the primary source of this blog’s S&P 500 EPS and revenue estimates and history.

Logic would tell us there is still more downside to stocks, with much dependent on crude oil prices. Brent should stay below the $115-122 level, and I’d like to see West Texas Intermediate (WTI) remain below $100.

The Friday before the Iran air strikes started, WTI closed that Friday, 2/27/26, at $67.02 per barrel.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: John Hancock 529 Enrollment-Based Portfolio Quarterly Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

• U.S. stocks extended a historically fast rebound that began in early April, although upward momentum slowed in the final quarter of the year.

• The advance was supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on AI, and the Fed's interest rate reductions of 0.25% in October and December.

• Healthcare, a laggard for much of 2025, was the top-performing sector in the S&P 500 Index during Q4.

Market review and outlook

U.S. stocks gained 2.66% in the fourth quarter, according to the S&P 500 Index, extending a historically fast rebound that began in early April, but at a slower pace. The advance was supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence, and the U.S. Federal Reserve's (Fed's) interest rate reductions of 0.25% in October and December. Amid this favorable backdrop for higher-risk assets, the index closed the year just shy of its all-time high. Value stocks modestly outpaced growth in Q4, while large caps had only a slight advantage over small caps, as investors' appetite for risk waned versus the prior three months.

In the fourth quarter, the index's narrow advance was driven by the defensive-oriented healthcare sector (+12%), which particularly shined in November. The growth-oriented communication services sector rose about 7%, benefiting from the transformative potential of generative AI. All other groups lagged the index. Notably, IT, which represented 35% of the S&P 500 Index in Q4, rose about 1%.

International developed-market stocks advanced 4.89%, as measured by the MSCI EAFE Index. The discrepancy between value and growth stocks was more pronounced in this index, again in favor of value over growth. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index notched a 4.72% rise. South Korea (+27%) and Taiwan (+10%) were two strong components with sizable representation in this index, bolstered by the strength in technology stocks in those markets. On the other hand, China finished the quarter with a roughly -7% return, giving back some of its gains since the April bottom.

U.S. taxable investment-grade bonds gained 1.1% in the fourth quarter, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. Quarterly performance was fueled by a combination of elevated starting yields and price appreciation, as bond prices rose amid falling interest rates and investor demand, supported by stable fundamentals.

The U.S. equity bull market is entering its fourth year, having put up double-digit gains in each of the past three, leaving investors wondering whether it can keep the streak alive. While multiyear stretches of positive equity returns are common, double-digit returns four years in row are few and far between. The naysayers point to elevated valuations, narrowness of the market, uncertainty around Fed policy amid upcoming leadership change, a softening labor market, and U.S. mid-term election risk. Yet, history has shown that bull markets don't die of old age alone, and while valuations are elevated, they're below prior cycle extremes. Bulls can also point to still-robust earnings expectations, a broadening of AI beneficiaries, fiscal support, increased capital spending, deregulation, rising mergers-and-acquisitions activity, and easing trade tensions. With the risks reasonably balanced and momentum still intact, the path of least resistance for the equity market may continue to be higher for now. The question is, can it reach double digits for the four-peat?

Contributors and detractors

The portfolio recorded a single-digit gain for the quarter and slightly trailed its blended benchmark, comprising a 64.75% weighting in the Russell 3000 Index, a 27.75% weighting in the MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (net) and a 7.50% weighting in the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. Disciplined Value International Fund (John Hancock) was a noteworthy detractor for the quarter. The fund's lower allocation to value stocks and higher allocation to growth versus the MSCI EAFE Value Index was a headwind in a quarter where international value equities strongly outperformed growth names. Disciplined Value Fund (John Hancock) also detracted. Stock selection in healthcare, communication services, industrials, and consumer discretionary proved disappointing for the quarter. Conversely, International Growth Fund (John Hancock) contributed on a relative basis, largely due to security selection in the financials sector. U.S. Growth Fund (John Hancock) also contributed, aided by stock selection in IT and industrials. A tactical overweight to international value equities further contributed, given the relatively strong performance of that group.

data.title: Virtus Income & Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
General Market Environment

Equities, convertible securities, and high yield (HY) bonds all advanced in the fourth quarter. Third-quarter earnings results beat expectations on strong artificial intelligence (AI) spending, though management commentary highlighted cost pressures and uneven demand trends. Economic data was mixed, and visibility was impacted by the government shutdown; labor market indicators softened, while consumer spending and inflation metrics were stable. The U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) cut rates by 25 basis points (bps) in both October and December and signaled further accommodation with markets pricing additional cuts into 2026. Against this backdrop, the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield finished modestly higher at 4.17% despite significant intra-quarter volatility.

Equities Market Environment

> The S&P 500® Index returned 2.66% for the quarter, bringing full-year performance to 17.88%.

> Almost all sectors finished higher with health care, communication services, and financials the top-performing sectors in the period, while real estate, utilities, and consumer staples were the bottom-performing sectors.

> Equity volatility peaked at 26.42 for the period but ultimately finished lower at 14.95.

Convertible Securities Market Environment

> The ICE BofA U.S. Convertible Index returned 1.97% for the quarter, bringing full-year performance to 17.98%.

> Sector performance was mostly higher with consumer staples, industrials, and healthcare outperforming, while financials, consumer discretionary, and media underperformed.

> Below-investment grade issues outperformed investment grade issues. Equity sensitive issues outperformed yield-oriented (busted) and total return (balanced) issues.

> New issuance saw 46 issues priced, raising $36.2 billion in proceeds, bringing the full-year total to a record $118.8 billion.

High Yield Bond Market Environment

> The ICE BofA U.S. High Yield Index returned 1.35% for the quarter, bringing full-year performance to 8.50%.

> BB, B, and CCC rated bonds returned 1.57%, 1.55%, and 0.52%, respectively.

> Spreads modestly widened to 281 bps from 280 bps, the average bond price fell to 98.06, and the market's yield rose to 7.08%.

> Industries were mostly positive for the period. Gaming, metals, and healthcare outperformed whereas packaging and paper, chemicals, and cable underperformed.

> Trailing 12-month default rates finished the period at 1.88% (par) and 1.40% (issues).

> Quarterly new issuance saw 75 issues priced, raising $65.4 billion in proceeds, bringing the full-year total to $332.0 billion.

Portfolio Commentary

The portfolio benefited from strength across equities, convertibles, and HY bonds.

Top contributors in the quarter were led by Alphabet Inc., which saw a favorable response to its newest AI model and optimism around plans to commercialize its proprietary chips. Apple Inc. was higher after reporting a top- and bottom-line beat and notable margin improvement, and a pharmaceutical company reached an agreement with the U.S. government to expand obesity drug access in exchange for favorable tariff treatment. Several other key beneficiaries of the AI buildout theme were positively impacted by strong earnings results, including Lumentum Holdings Inc., a semiconductor manufacturer, and a data center operator. The other top contributors were a precision oncology company, a heavy equipment maker, and a biotechnology holding.

Top detractors in the period consisted of Microsoft Corp., a social media holding, and a cloud technology company that were all hampered by reservations around elevated capital expenditure. A retailer was lower on home improvement spending prospects, a rideshare operator indicated near-term margin headwinds due to strategic investment initiatives, and an e-commerce company saw modest deceleration in its internet retail business. The other top detractors in the period included a streaming services provider on margin concerns in addition to merger and acquisition headlines and a cryptocurrency exchange, among others.

Many option positions expired below strike and the portfolio was able to retain the set premiums.

Exposure increased the most in healthcare, industrials, and utilities, and decreased the most in consumer discretionary, technology, and communication services. Covered call option positioning increased quarter over quarter.

This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Pentagon prepares for potential ground operations in Iran - WaPo

The Trump administration has sent mixed signals about the conflict’s direction, with President Trump expressing interest in negotiations while White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt warned that the president is “prepared to unleash hell” if Iran does not abandon its nuclear ambitions. Trump himself denied plans to deploy ground troops, telling reporters, “I’m not putting troops anywhere.”

Houthis claim first attack on Israel since Iran war began

If Houthis attack commercial vessels as they have done in the past, there could be disruption to global trade through the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, an alternative route for getting crude from Saudi Arabia to Asia amid Iran’s near stranglehold on the Strait of Hormuz.

Senate advances Homeland Security funding bill as shutdown strains airports

Mounting disruptions at airports, including long security lines and staffing shortages as unpaid TSA workers called out sick or resigned, increased pressure on lawmakers to reach a deal. Major hubs such as Atlanta, Houston and New York saw extended wait times, with lines stretching across terminals and even outside in some cases.

SA Analyst OANDA Group said a sustained move above $102.25 for WTI could trigger a fresh bullish leg toward $111–$124, while a break below $85.50 would invalidate the bullish view and expose downside toward the $81–$73 zone.

Oil industry executives paint grim picture of Iran war supply disruption

At the S&P Global's annual CERAWeek energy conference, some executives were concerned with the Trump administration's optimistic messaging and said price gyrations and the uncertainty hanging over the conflict make it all but impossible to plan investments.

data.title: CPZ: Complex Portfolio Isn't Working As Intended
data.description:
Overview

As you become a more advanced investor, you may start to incorporate different investment strategies within your portfolio. For instance, it isn't unusual for investors to include some short positions as a hedge against market pullbacks. Investors can also hold preferred securities as a way to provide some stability during market uncertainty. However, the Calamos Long/Short Equity & Dynamic Income Trust (CPZ) eliminates the need for investors to manually complete these complex strategies on their own. The fund aims to provide attractive total returns while utilizing a long/short pairing alongside a fixed-income portion. The different strategies work together to provide a well-balanced portfolio that can cater towards risk -averse investors.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that CPZ's share price has declined by about 13.3%. Even when including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return still sits at a loss of nearly 3.4% over the same time frame. CPZ now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 12.5%, while issuing those payouts on a monthly basis. However, I believe that the payouts are a bit too generous at this time and this has caused the coverage rate to be quite inconsistent over the last few years.

Although the high yield may be appealing for income investors, there are some tradeoffs to its strategy. For instance, the fund has frequently faced the scenario where it paid out more than it actually earned, which contributed to NAV erosion during certain periods. Furthermore, the historical data indicates that the fund's short strategy hasn't protected investors from losses and it may be negatively impacting CPZ's ability to participate in strong market rallies. So let's start by reviewing the fund's strategy that it uses to generate earnings for shareholders.

Inefficient Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, CPZ has managed assets of $437.7M that are spread across a diverse blend of assets. CPZ's portfolio strategy is very actively managed so the fund has a high expense ratio of 3.05%. However, when excluding costs related to leverage and operating costs, the actual management fee sits at 1.10% of assets. CPZ doesn't track a passive index and instead prioritizes monthly income while having a low correlation to the broad market. The blended approach is made up of the following breakdown:

  • Long/Short Component: 69.2%
  • Fixed-Income Component: 16.4%
  • Preferred Component: 14.5%

The long exposure makes up 75.3% of the portfolio, while the short exposure makes up 67.1% of its equities portion. This unique breakdown can be attributed to the fund's use of leverage, which I will revisit later in this article. Since CPZ doesn't try to track an index, the positions are selected based on management's fundamental analysis on securities across the U.S and Europe that have the potential for alpha. Management focuses on long positions that have strong cash flows, while finding short positions that have deteriorating metrics.

When looking at the equity portion of its portfolio, we can see that the top five long positions are focused on industrials. For instance, the Boeing Company (BA) is the largest position at 3.9%. This is followed by an allocation to other notable positions, including United Airlines Holdings (UAL), L3Harris Technologies, Canadian Pacific Kansas City (CP) and Wells Fargo (WFC).

When looking at the short strategy, we can see that CPZ is most heavily allocated to the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) at 42.7%. So if CPZ is short the S&P 500, you would assume that the fund will be able to perform while the indices continue to fall, right? However, a quick comparison of CPZ against SPY over the last six months, we can see that CPZ severely underperforms. This makes me question whether or not the short strategy is actually effective over time.

Another aspect of the portfolio is the fixed-income portion that aims to generate income independent of market conditions. The structure mixes in debt securities with varying credit ratings and locations across the globe. The fixed-income assets help create a foundation to support the monthly distributions, but it introduces some risks related to interest rate sensitivity. The portfolio's breakdown by asset type is as follows:

  • High Yield Corporate Debt: 72.1%
  • Syndicated Loans: 15%
  • Investment Grade: 9.4%
  • Equity: 0.6%
  • Preferred Shares: 0.3%
  • Securitized Debt: 0.2%

When looking at the preferred securities, CPZ chooses to take a focus across the financial industry. For instance, Wells Fargo remains the largest position here. This is followed by an allocation to JPMorgan (JPM), Liberty Mutual Group, Ares Finance Company (ARES), Capital One Financial (COF), just to name a few.

Valuation & Performance Risk

Since CPZ is a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of its underlying assets. Following the recent pullback in share price, CPZ currently trades at a discount to NAV of 11.7%. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that CPZ now trades somewhere in the middle of its historical price to NAV range. For reference, CPZ has traded at an average discount to NAV of 10.36% over the last five years. However, I wouldn't consider this larger-than-average discount valuation an automatic sign to accumulate shares. I believe that the growing discount is a direct reflection of the fund's inefficiencies.

To support this, I wanted to review the latest available annual report for the 2025 period. Net investment income amounted to $0.25 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.74 per share. Looking back on prior years, we can see that CPZ's net investment income falls within its typical range, excluding the strong performance in 2024. However, net investment income hasn't historically been strong enough to support distributions and NAV growth on its own, which means that CPZ is very dependent on net realized gains to bridge this gap in earnings.

The issue is that net realized gains can wildly vary and this makes it hard to estimate how the fund will perform during different market cycles. For instance, market indices raced to all-time highs throughout 2025, but CPZ was unable to participate in this growth since its net realized gains were only $0.74 per share, which falls below the prior year's net realized gains of $2.20 per share. As a result, CPZ's underlying NAV growth is quite disappointing as the fund has failed to regain enough momentum to get back above its 2021 high. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $21.52 per share
  • 2022: $17.66 per share
  • 2023: $16.54 per share
  • 2024: $17.53 per share
  • 2025: $16.64 per share

My thoughts here are that the fund's short positions are actively working against its ability to grow during bull markets. When indices are rising, CPZ is forced to take losses on these short positions and that is the main issue here. Statistically, the S&P 500 rises more often than it falls, so a position in CPZ is best utilized as a temporary strategic position more than a buy-and-hold fund. As previously established, this complex dynamic hasn't successfully offset losses over the last few months.

Furthermore, the strategy has also led CPZ to underperform the S&P 500 since its inception. For instance, CPZ's total return since inception sits at 14.16%, which is far below SPY's total return of 71.5% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors need to understand that a high yield doesn't automatically result in a high total return over time.

One of the biggest risks for CPZ is the aggressive use of leverage that the fund uses in an attempt to amplify its earnings and support the short positions. The total effective leverage now amounts to 51.31% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$317.8M. This is an aggressive use of leverage that significantly increases the overall risk profile of the fund since interest rates remain elevated. High interest rates means that the fund's operating spread between the cost of its leverage and the yield collected from securities are thin. Furthermore, leverage can increase losses if allocated to positions that don't perform as anticipated.

So let's suppose that CPZ uses some leverage to hold short positions. This would mean that CPZ would see large losses on the short positions if the S&P 500 were to race to new all-time highs. There is the potential for leverage to be less risky when interest rates are eventually lowered. However, the Fed decided to keep interest rates unchanged during their last meeting, so there's no telling how much longer rates will remain elevated.

Not only does the leverage introduce risks related to interest rates, but the exposure to fixed-income assets also introduces risks related to defaults. Since interest rates remain high, the debt markets have continued to deteriorate. Since CPZ has exposure to below-investment grade credit ratings, the fund can also be negatively impacted by borrower defaults. For instance, a significant portion of assets are allocated to securities with poor credit ratings. The breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 10.2%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 87.3%
  • Unrated: 2.6%

Inconsistent Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.14 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 12.5%. Assuming the fund pays out a similar distribution over the next twelve months, this equates to an annual payout of $1.68 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, the fund's dividend coverage is very inconsistent and is regularly faced with the issue of paying out more than it actually earns. For instance, the fund only generated total earnings of $0.99 per share, which was far below the level needed to support payouts. As the fund continues to pay out more than it earns, the underlying NAV is likely to continue eroding over time.

Looking at the payout history below, we can see that CPZ has decided to keep payouts relatively unchanged since inception. However, I would argue that the yield is simply too generous right now and it is hurting the total return potential. If the distributions were reduced by 25%, the dividend yield would still be around 9.4%, which remains competitive amongst the high yield landscape. A lower payout would improve the fund's operating spreads, increase cash flows that can be reinvested into growth, and create a steadier NAV over time.

Lastly, investors need to consider that the dividends received from CPZ may not offer any tax efficiency. Since the distributions are being supported by the combination of net investment income and net realized gains, the majority of payouts are likely classified as ordinary dividends, which have the highest tax burden for investors. Therefore, a position in CPZ would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, CPZ is a bit of a tricky fund to recommend. On one end, the complex strategy can be a solid option for investors that want to hedge against market declines. However, the fund has failed to outperform the S&P 500 over the last six months, despite its short positions and fixed-income securities that can serve as a hedge. In fact, the unique inclusion of fixed-income investments is likely one of the reasons why the share price has struggled. As the debt market continues to deteriorate under the pressure of high interest rates, this has caused CPZ's operating spread to shrink. Furthermore, the inclusion of a short strategy sounds great but it actively limits returns during market rallies.

data.title: ClearBridge Appreciation Portfolios Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

• Markets: The S&P 500 Index extended its advance in the fourth quarter, rising 2.7% and finishing the year up 17.9%. Following the “Liberation Day” selloff, the index posted gains in each subsequent month (eight consecutive monthly advances), climbing 37% from the April 8 market low. While market participation broadened in the fourth quarter, 2025 was again dominated by mega cap technology and the AI ecosystem.

• Contributors: Stock selection in health care, consumer staples and consumer discretionary proved beneficial.

• Detractors: Stock selection in the IT, communication services, industrials and materials sectors detracted from relative results.

• Outlook: We remain focused on through-the-cycle outperformance via downside protection, and we increasingly believe risks may be starting to outweigh the prospective rewards.

Performance Review

• The ClearBridge Appreciation Portfolios underperformed the benchmark S&P 500 Index in the fourth quarter. On an absolute basis, the Portfolios had positive contributions from six of 11 sectors. The health care and communication services sectors were the main positive contributors, while industrials, materials and IT were the main detractors.

• On an individual stock basis, the biggest relative contributors during the quarter were Eli Lilly, Alphabet (GOOGL), Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO), ASML (ASML) and an underweight to Oracle (ORCL). The biggest detractors were Netflix (NFLX), Eaton (ETN), Micron Technology (MU) (not owned), Microsoft (MSFT) and Automatic Data Processing (ADP).

• Eli Lilly shares were up as the U.S. government struck a deal with Lilly to offer GLP-1s to Medicare and Medicaid patients, opening up millions of additional prescriptions, while readouts on the company’s oral GLP-1 treatment indicated a broader market than we had expected.

• Alphabet’s success with its Google Gemini chatbot and TPU chips for AI workloads boosted its shares more than 60% for 2025; in addition, the DOJ antitrust case concluded with less onerous penalties than feared, helping sentiment.

• Netflix (NFLX) fell as it became involved in the highly publicized bidding process to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD). We see merits to the deal for Netflix to buy WBD’s streaming and studio assets at the currently agreed price, but investors are concerned that a bidding war might ensue should Paramount Skydance further raise its competing offer. If Netflix’s current offer prevails, we view the deal as a net positive — while there is some execution risk, we see better revenue and cost synergies than the stock move is implying.

• Eaton, a manufacturer of electrical components critical to the operation of data centers, has supply constraints that limit its ability to vastly exceed its targeted growth rate and that may also limit its ability to garner price increases.

Outlook

• Like in the past year, policy today remains supportive of near-term growth, and capital markets conditions suggest risk assets — such as stocks — may continue to perform. But for the first time in the post-COVID period, we see pockets of weakness that could result in a more challenging economic backdrop.

• Given signs that markets may be entering a more speculative phase, we believe the probability of a correction (a peak-to-trough decline of >10%) has risen, and the possibility a selloff could prove more prolonged than in recent years makes the “buy the dip” mentality more precarious. In our view, capital markets will determine whether equities can sustain their advance. If investors continue to reward AI-related capex with higher share prices the cycle can persist. However, if markets tire of new multiyear infrastructure commitments or funding AI firms at ever richer valuations, there is little valuation support to fall back on. With half of consumers already showing signs of strain, we view any correction as posing more risk than any time in recent memory.

• We remain focused on through-the-cycle outperformance via downside protection, and we increasingly believe risks may be starting to outweigh the prospective rewards. Accordingly, we’re keeping a close eye on balance sheet and cash flow durability across our AI-exposed holdings to ensure our exposure is concentrated in companies with the financial wherewithal to weather a potential cooling in today’s white-hot environment.

data.title: Columbia Seligman Global Technology Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

  • Institutional Class shares of Columbia Seligman Global Technology Fund returned 4.96% for the fourth quarter.
  • The fund's benchmark, the MSCI World Information Technology Index - Net (the index) returned 1.37% for the same period.
  • Stock selection within semiconductors & semiconductor equipment; technology hardware, storage & peripherals; and electronic equipment, instruments & components contributed positively to performance relative to the benchmark. The fund's underweight allocation to software also contributed to relative performance, as did out-of-index allocations to electrical equipment within the industrials sector and hotels, restaurants & leisure within the consumer discretionary sector.
  • For monthly performance, please visit Financial Professionals | Columbia Threadneedle Investments US .

Market overview

U.S. equities posted a gain of 2.41% during the fourth quarter, as measured by the Russell 1000 Index. The Index returned 17.37% in 2025, marking the third year in a row in which it gained 15% or more, as well as the sixth of the last seven years. The positive fourth-quarter return was primarily a function of the same factors that propelled stocks over the full year — better-than-expected economic growth, robust corporate earnings results and inflation that largely remained below an annualized rate of 3%. The U.S. Federal Reserve continued to ease policy, cutting rates by a quarter point at its meetings in October and December and announcing an end to the multi-year effort to reduce the size of its balance sheet. Stocks also remained supported by ongoing excitement surrounding the artificial intelligence (AI) theme, albeit with a brief stretch of concern in November that AI-related equities were in a “bubble.” Notably, the quarter was characterized by a broadening of leadership away from the mega-cap technology stocks that had been key drivers of market performance for most of the year.

Top holdings (% of net assets): as of December 31, 2025

Bloom Energy (BE) 8.18
Lam Research (LRCX) 7.08
NVIDIA (NVDA) 5.83
Alphabet-A (GOOGL) 4.85
Broadcom (AVGO) 4.85
Microsoft (MSFT) 4.28
Western Digital (WDC) 3.67
Apple (AAPL) 3.55
Marvell Technology (MRVL) 3.25
Applied Materials (AMAT) 3.25
Top holdings exclude short-term holdings and cash, if applicable. Fund holdings are as of the date given, are subject to change at any time, and are not recommendations to buy or sell any security.

Quarterly portfolio recap

▪ Technology indices continued to move higher during the quarter as evidenced by the MSCI World Information Technology Index which returned 1.37% for the quarter. The quarter was not a totally smooth ride, and there were pockets of volatility that the technology market had not felt since headline tariff volatility in April. The primary driver of volatility during the quarter was declining sentiment in the "AI trade" and whether the significant AI capital expenditure (capex) spend had ramped up too fast for what the market was expecting. This led to a sharp sell-off in AI-indexed companies. However, the market ultimately settled, following a very strong earnings report from Micron (MU) that helped ease some concerns around the AI-capex race.

▪ The fund maintained an underweight position in NVIDIA relative to the MSCI World Information Technology Index. The chip manufacturing company still maintained a large allocation in the benchmark, at an average weight of over 8.4% during the quarter. The fund's underweight position contributed to performance relative to the benchmark. NVIDIA continued to deliver strong results, including an earnings beat during the quarter and an upbeat outlook. However, the market reacted cautiously following the earnings report, as investors digested margin compression and whether the future upward guidance had already been priced into the strong run the stock has had since 2023.

▪ The portfolio's holding in semiconductor equipment manufacturer Lam Research contributed significantly to positive performance, as the stock performed exceptionally well during the quarter and the fund maintained a significant relative overweight. Lam continued to be an AI beneficiary from a company perspective, as AI workloads require more advanced chips, which drove demand for Lam's deposition and etch solutions. The market has shown potential for a memory "supercycle," which has led to significant investments in high-bandwidth memory and AI chips and is fueling demand for Lam's wafer fabrication equipment.

▪ The fund continued to hold an out-of-benchmark position in Bloom Energy, which manufactures and markets solid-oxide fuel cells that produce electricity and can provide an alternative source of energy compared with traditional supply. Our thesis on Bloom has been that it has the technology to provide a solution to the electricity shortage that overhangs new AI-data-center builds in the U.S. and around the world. Bloom's fuel cells plug into a natural gas line that can fit on a data center's campus without taking up much real estate, and the company has informed customers that it can ramp up power delivery quicker than other energy providers. Bloom's stock price did pull back at times during the quarter, especially as a group of higher-beta stocks faced heightened volatility behind the fear of an overspend on AI buildouts. We continue to hold high conviction in Bloom Energy, especially with the clear shortage in reliable power solutions to power AI data centers going forward. Jensen Huang, NVIDIA's CEO, has said multiple times that power is the largest constraint on AI; as a result, there has been a clear demand for power solutions that can be scaled up quickly and efficiently.

▪ The fund maintained an overweight position in technology hardware, storage & peripherals company Western Digital. Early in 2025, the company announced a strategic spin-off separating its flash division as SanDisk, while Western Digital focused primarily on the hard disk drive (HDD) market, allowing the company to be valued as a standaloneHDD leader. The continued surge in AI infrastructure demand massively boosted the demand for high-capacity HDDs, and Western Digital benefited from an increase in purchase orders from major hyperscalers extending into 2026 and even 2027.

▪ The fund's allocations to semiconductor equipment companies Teradyne and Applied Materials contributed positively to performance during the quarter, following a strong overall period for semiconductor capital equipment companies. Teradyne's strong price appreciation was led by an increased demand for semiconductor testing equipment, fueled by booming demand for AI accelerators and memory. Its management team also reported strong results for the third quarter of 2025 and issued optimistic guidance for future business with significant revenue growth year over year. Applied Materials' management team emphasized the continued shift toward AI-related chip production, including foundry logic, dynamic random-access memory (DRAM) and high-bandwidth memory - all areas in which the company holds strong positioning. Applied Materials also expanded their gross margins during the quarter, citing some of the strongest margins it has seen in approximately 25 years.

Outlook

The market is navigating significant crosscurrents of positive and negative trends. Following two strong quarters for overall market returns, and technology specifically, volatility did increase slightly during the fourth quarter, but fundamentals remained strong overall. Positive market trends include:

▪ AI infrastructure build-out remains strong, as hyperscalers and enterprises are committing to large-scale spends on graphics processing units (GPUs), high-speed networking and high-bandwidth memory.

▪ Corporate technology refresh and digitization are intact, as large-cap companies continue to invest in cloud, security, data platforms and automation.

▪ Storage and memory pricing and margins have improved, following a down cycle in 2022 and 2023. Hard-disk drives and NAND/DRAM markets tightened in 2025 on AI data demand, lifting prices and margins within the subsector.

The negatives include:

▪ Semiconductor supply chains remain exposed to export controls and geopolitical tensions, given the current environment.

▪ While AI demand is strong, graphics processing unit (GPU) and high bandwidth memory (HBM) costs are rising, tightening margins for cloud providers and downstream integrators.

▪ Increased regulatory scrutiny on data privacy, AI ethics and antitrust could be a headwind for cybersecurity, as companies look for more solutions amid increasing AI adoption.

Our expectations for various sectors are as follows:

▪ Favorable Fed policy with the potential for additional rate cuts could support capital spending and technology investment in growth segments like AI and semiconductors.

▪ Semiconductor equipment demand remains steady and has continued to recover strongly, following increased volatility in April 2025 around global tariffs. Semiconductor capital equipment companies have been focused on reallocating production across geographic locations to adjust for potential impacts of tariffs if conversations restart.

▪ Cybersecurity consistently remains a top priority for chief information officer (CIO) budgets, as non-technology-focused companies continue to increase the use of AI solutions in their day-to-day operations.

data.title: Fidus Investment: Contrarian Buy At 52-Week Lows
data.description:
Fidus Investment (FDUS) has suffered greatly in 2026, as a confluence of factors weighed on the BDC sector, including growing fears over the performance of loans made to the SaaS sector as well as the recent outbreak of war in the Middle East. Amid the confrontation with Iran, the Federal Reserve did not lower rates in March which may ultimately benefit the BDC sector as a whole. Fidus Investment as a result has dropped to 52-week lows which creates a contrarian buying opportunity for investors, in my opinion. Most importantly, because consumer prices have been rising lately, prospects of a rate increase have actually improved which would benefit BDCs with variable rate-paying loans in their portfolio.

A long history of steady loan expansion, growing NII

Fidus Investment is a variable rate-focused BDC that originates loans to the middle market: about 75.3% of all investments bore variable rates as of Q4'25. The investment firm had a portfolio valued at $1.3B as of the end of the December quarter and it consisted chiefly of first liens. These loans, which are the most heavily protected in the capital structure, represented 78.1% of investments for the BDC in the fourth-quarter, while other investments includes second lien debt, subordinated debt and equity.

Fidus Investment, like so many investment platforms in the last decade, benefited from growing private credit demand after the financial crisis in 2008 during which traditional banks withdrew from lending. As a result, the investment company has benefited from steady demand for privately-originated debt and equity capital which has led Fidus Investment to publish an all-time record portfolio volume as of the end of the fourth-quarter.

As per the BDC's latest statement of income, Fidus Investment generated $19.6M in net investment income, showing a year-over-year growth rate of 5%. Since many BDCs reported a shrinking of their portfolio values in Q4'25, on a year-over-year basis, as well as falling NII, Fidus Investment did actually quite well here with a positive growth rate.

Improving dividend coverage trend translates to low-risk dividend

In terms of actual coverage performance, the BDC managed to support is dividend with net investment income. Fidus Investment does not only pay a regular dividend, but has adopted a multi-layered distribution strategy that combines a base dividend of $0.43 per share with supplemental dividend income that depends on the level of adjusted net investment income (the level of NII after recognition of capital gains incentive fees).

In the most recent quarter, Fidus Investment had a coverage ratio of 106% which was the first quarter in the last year that the BDC exceeded its NII per-share. Therefore, I see very little risk for the BDC right now to resort to a dividend cut, especially now that the odds of a rate increase have actually increased lately.

A buy at 52-week lows, exaggerated 13% discount to NAV

The BDC has seen its share price drop to a 52-week low last week on growing fears of an escalating war in the Middle East that has engulfed not only the U.S., Israel and Iran, but also many other Gulf countries. Persistent fears about the disruptive impact of artificial intelligence technologies on the software loan portfolios of BDCs in the private credit market have further weighed on investor sentiment. In addition, other BDCs like FS KKR Capital (FSK), Golub Capital BDC (GBDC) and New Mountain Finance (NMFC) all lowered their dividends this year in anticipation of lower base rates in the U.S. economy.

Shares of Fidus Investment are priced at 0.87X net asset value which gives the mid-size BDC a higher valuation factor, based off of NAV, than Blue Owl Capital (OBDC), one of the biggest private credit platforms in the market. Ares Capital (ARCC), the biggest BDC in the industry by market cap, has a P/NAV ratio of 0.88X which is followed by Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), which has a price-to-NAV ratio of 0.87X.

Right now, investors don't seem to be rational about the assessment of BDCs, which creates a lot of bargains in the industry, Fidus Investment being just one of them. I am currently overweighting investment platforms that trade at 10%+ discounts and one I am adding right now is Fidus Investment: the 13% discount to NAV seems exaggerated considering that the BDC currently fully supports its total dividend payout, and Fidus Investment has the opportunity to scale back variable dividend payments in order to avoid a permanent dividend cut, if it were to experience a dividend shortfall. A revaluation to at least 1.0X NAV (fair value: $19.55) is therefore a distinct possibility, in my opinion, especially given that the BDC is actually still growing its net investment income.

Risks of Fidus Investment

In the short term, the biggest risk for Fidus Investment is clearly a deterioration of investor sentiment with regard to SaaS loans which may continue to weigh on the sector for a while longer. Secondly, a drop in the dividend coverage ratio below 90% would be a major red flag for me, especially because so many other BDCs have already lowered their dividends in expectation of falling base rates in 2026. What would change my outlook on Fidus Investment is if the company were to see a decline in its loan origination growth or if its NII started to decline.

Closing thoughts

The value proposition for Fidus Investment may be better than investors realize: the recent surge in consumer prices, especially in the energy sector, after the outbreak of a new war in the Middle East, is pressuring the Federal Reserve to actually start to consider interest rate increases. This marks a significant departure from last year's consensus expectation that 2026 will see only Federal Fund rate cuts.

Rate increases may be an unexpected present for the BDC sector in general as well as Fidus Investment which will benefit from rising base rates. While I expect interest rates to trend down longer term, the current setup for BDCs (heavily oversold sentiment, multiple BDCs trading at 52-week lows, soaring investor pessimism, double-digit discounts to NAV) creates a strong engagement setup for me. I believe contrarian-minded investors may want to see the current sector panic as an investment opportunity and double down on leading BDC plays with growing NII, like Fidus Investment.
